---
title: "Presentation: Arts Education Nonprofit Landscape"
author: "Steven Todd McIntyre II"
image: "header.jpg"
date: "2026-03-11"
runtime: shiny
format: 
  dashboard:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: minty
    output:
      orientation: columns
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(here)
library(knitr)
library(shiny)
library(rio)
library(plotly)
library(usmap)
library(scales)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggbeeswarm)

```

# Overview

## Column

**How useful can NTEE Codes be in Studying Arts Education Nonprofits?**

This dashboard will explore United States nonprofit organizations that are coded as "A25: Arts Education" in the [National Taxonomy of Exempt Organizations (NTEE)](https://nccs.urban.org/nccs/pubs/ntee-two-page-2005.pdf), a coding scheme that describes kinds of nonprofit organizations. "A25" is defined by the Urban Institute as "Organizations that provide informal arts educational programming and/or instruction but do not grant diplomas or degrees; or which offer services regarding the arts to educational institutions or to public entities involved in education." Using data from the [Urban Institute's Business Master File (BMF)](https://urbaninstitute.github.io/nccs/datasets/bmf/), I explore the kinds of organizations that define themselves as A25 and consider the NTEE classification's usefulness in exploring nonprofit arts education activities.

# Visualization 1: Arts Education Nonprofit Title Word Count

## Column

```{r}
a25 <- import(here("data/BMF_A25.csv"))

# set categories of top 50 words
kind_of_org <- c("foundation", "institute", "school", "center", "academy", "association", "guild", "society", "project", "studio", "incorporated", "club", "corporation", "alliance", "productions", "program", "council", "collective", "nfp", "league", "friends", "corp", "boosters", "gallery")
art_words <- c("arts", "art", "music", "creative", "fine", "design", "theatre", "performing", "workshop", "graphic", "dance", "traditional", "cultural", "hooking", "theater", "quilters", "woodturners")
education_words <- c("education", "learning", "educational")
locations <- c("american", "community", "international", "county", "valley", "city")
target_audience <- c("artists", "youth", "kids", "artist", "childrens")

# remove orgs that no longer exist
a25 <- a25 |>
  filter(ORG_YEAR_LAST >= 2024)

# create a table of the top 50 words in org names
a25_top_50 <- a25 |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = ORG_NAME_CURRENT) |>
  select(word) |>
  filter(!word %in% stop_words$word) |>
  count(word) |>
  mutate(word = fct_lump_n(word, n=50, w=n)) |>
  filter(word != "Other") |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarize(n = sum(n)) |>
  mutate(word=fct_reorder(word, n))

# add categories to the top 50 words
a25_top_50 <- a25_top_50 |>
  mutate(category = case_when(
    word%in%kind_of_org ~ "organization",
    word%in%art_words ~ "art",
    word%in%education_words ~ "education",
    word%in%locations ~ "location",
    word%in%target_audience ~ "people"
  ))

# create plot
a25_top_50_plot <- a25_top_50 |>
  ggplot(aes(n, word, fill = category)) +
  geom_col(width = .9, position = position_dodge(.7)) +
  labs(
    title = "Top 50 Words in Arts Education Nonprofit Names",
    x = "Instances in Organization Names",
    y = "",
    fill = "Word Category",
    caption = "Data from the Urban Institute's Business Master File. Categories coded by author."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8) 
  ) +
  scale_fill_discrete(palette = "Set2")

ggplotly(a25_top_50_plot)
```

## Sidebar {.sidebar}

**Commentary** There are a number of NTEE codes under which nonprofits that engage in art education might land:

-   A40: Visual Arts Orgs
-   A50-57: Museums
-   A60-A6F: Performing Arts Orgs
-   B\*: Education Orgs
-   O\*: Youth Development Orgs

Arts education exists underneath in wide capacities under these kinds of orgs. I wanted to know what kinds of organizations primarily code themselves as "A25: Arts Education" and this word count was very informative to that effect. I coded each word into a category based on the "kind" of word it is, and was surprised by "hooking," which represents a set of chapters of largely geriatric organizations committed to a form of rug-making.

At this point, I started to realize that this landscape includes K-12 and postsecondary education, but also adult and continuing education organizations. I also realized that my initial hope--comparing arts education nonprofit funding and activity with public arts education funding and activity--isn't as simple, on the nonprofit side, as filtering for NTEE_CODE == "A25" and calling it a day. There is a lot of complexity within and without "A25" that makes 1-1 comparisons of public and private funding in localities that focus only on "A25" incomplete at best, misleading at worst.

**Process Notes:**

This was one of the more challenging sets to wrangle data. I got used to the `tidytext` package, learned about so-called "stop words" (e.g., "a", "the", "of", etc.), and also learned about my data. Turns out, my dataset includes all data from all time so I had to filter for nonprofits that still exist.

# Visualization 2: US Map

## Column

```{r}
# create table with count of A25 in each state
a25_state_count <- a25 |>
  count(F990_ORG_ADDR_STATE) |>
  mutate(state = F990_ORG_ADDR_STATE) |>
  select(state, n) |>
  filter(state != "")

# create map plot using US Map
a25_dist_map <- plot_usmap(data = a25_state_count, values = "n") +
  labs(title = "Where are the Arts Education Nonprofits?",
       subtitle = "Count of arts education nonprofits in each US state.",
       fill = "Number of Arts\nEducation Nonprofits",
       caption = "Data from the Urban Institute's Business Master File.") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_continuous(palette = "PuBu")

ggplotly(a25_dist_map)

```

## Sidebar {.sidebar}

**Commentary**

I don't have as much to say here, as while this is a pretty-to-look-at representation of where these exist, it was relatively to implement (googling "how do I do x" seems to lead to easy-to-use packages, like, in this case, `usmap`) and is, unfortunately, pretty much a population heat map.

It does lead nicely into my next visualization....

# Visualization 3: Arts Education Nonprofits vs. State Population

## Column {.tabset}

```{r}
#| title: "Static, With Labels"
# join usmaps' state population table w/ a25 count data
a25_statepop <- inner_join(a25_state_count, statepop, by = join_by(state == abbr))

# create scatterplot with fit line, highlight higher and lower states
a25_statepop_scatter <- a25_statepop |>
  ggplot(aes(x=pop_2022, y=n, label=state)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "grey90") +
  geom_point(color = "grey60") +
  geom_point(data = a25_statepop |> filter(state%in%c("CA", "NY", "OR", "MA", "VT", "DC")), color = "green") +
  geom_point(data = a25_statepop |> filter(state%in%c("PR", "MS", "SC", "NE", "OK")), color = "red") +
  geom_text_repel(color = "black") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Variation of Arts Education Nonprofits vs. State Population",
    subtitle = "Count of arts education nonprofits in each US state. Note states above and below the line. Any trends?",
    x = "2022 State Population (log10)",
    y = "Number of Arts Education Nonprofits (log10)",
    caption = "Nonprofit data from the Urban Institute's Business Master File. State population data provided through \`usmap\` package.\nEach are plotted logarithmically for better spacing between lower-populated states."
    
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c()) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_log10()

a25_statepop_scatter
```

```{r}
#| title: "Interactive, No Labels"
#make ggplotly version
ggplotly(a25_statepop_scatter)
```

## Sidebar {.sidebar}

**Commentary**

This was fun to examine--looking at the population of states vs. count of arts education nonprofits in that state. I noticed a sort of political trend here, which wasn't exactly what I expected. Note that, above the fit line, most states are blue states, and below, most are red state.

I was a bit surprised by Puerto Rico being so low, but I wonder what the nonprofit culture looks like in PR broadly. Would this graph look similar if it incorporated all kinds of nonprofits?

**Process**

We talked a bit about how logs are not intuitive, and I tend to agree. However, the nature of my data let me to using them in this and my next visualization.

Highlighting the most higher than expected and the most lower than expected seemed reasonable.

I have both a static version with labels, which is a bit loud but I really like to examine trends, and a `ggplotly` version with no labels that you can gover, which looks cleaner but isn't as easy to examine trends.

# Visualization 4: 2024 Arts Education Nonprofit Revenues

## Column {.tabset}

```{r}
#| title: "Log10 Distribution"
# Make Distribution Beeswarm Plot
a25_rev_distr_log <- a25 |>
  ggplot(aes(y="", x = F990_TOTAL_REVENUE_RECENT, label = ORG_NAME_CURRENT)) +
  geom_quasirandom(color = "darkgrey") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_currency(prefix = "$", big.mark = ",")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Revenue Distribution for Arts Education Nonprofits",
    y = "",
    x = "Total Revenue Reported in 2024 F990 Form (log10)",
    caption = "Nonprofit data from the Urban Institute's Business Master File."
  )

ggplotly(a25_rev_distr_log)
```

```{r}
#| title: "Linear Distribution"
a25_rev_distr_lin <- a25 |>
  ggplot(aes(y="", x = F990_TOTAL_REVENUE_RECENT, label = ORG_NAME_CURRENT)) +
  geom_quasirandom(color = "darkgrey") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_currency(prefix = "$", big.mark = ",")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "2024 Revenue Distribution for Arts Education Nonprofits",
    y = "",
    x = "Total Revenue Reported in 2024 F990 Form",
    caption = "Nonprofit data from the Urban Institute's Business Master File."
  )

ggplotly(a25_rev_distr_lin)
```

## Sidebar {.sidebar}

**Commentary**

Counts of arts education nonprofits is fun, but I was curious about the spread of what kinds of *revenues* these organizations bring in. This could speak to their levels of impact.

Near the top, we have a Head Start provider in South Los Angeles, CalArts, perhaps the premiere private arts university in the US, and Interlochen, a summer camp/arts education center in the midwest. The latter two make a lot of sense (though I might expect CalArts to be under B40: Higher Education), but a Head Start program being listed under Arts Education a bit surprising to me when B21: Preschools and O20: Youth Centers and Clubs exist.

I think this speaks to the self-selecting nature of NTEE codes and how they reflect how organizations conceptualize themselves at time of founding, since NTEE codes are determined when they first apply for nonprofit status.

**Process**

I tried a boxplot, but I wanted a more nuanced look at the distribution. I tried a violin plot, but it didn't allow for specific outliers. Then, I learned about the beeswarm plot from a Google search (`ggbeeswarm`) and really like it for my purpose--it is, in my opinion, a version of a jitterplot that better captures density. Alongside `ggplotly`, this allows for an at-a-glance distribution that the user can hover for specific information.

I included both the linear distribution and a log10 distribution. The former to tell a story and the latter for an easier to look at and explore distribution, assuming the audience knows log10. As a general rule, the further I go through visualizations, the more technical of an audience I'm expecting, so I think using log10 is fine.

# Summary of Updates

## Column

This pass, pretty much just added some context in sidebars for each viz!
